coredata.dc:description: Tin (IV) sulfide is a promising anode active material for lithium ion batteries due to its relatively high reversible capacity of 644 mAh/g, which is more than one and a half times that of graphite. During lithiation of tin (IV) sulfide, an inert Li2S matrix is formed in the first discharge cycle, which serves to accommodate the mechanical stresses associated with the volume expansion of tin during the successive LixSn alloying and de-alloying reactions. In order to improve the electrochemical performance of tin (IV) sulfide further, fundamental understanding and insights into the thermodynamics, phase formation, and driving forces for the lithiation reactions are still required. Therefore, in this work, a computational thermodynamics approach was combined with ex-situ XRD investigations of electrodes during the discharge reaction as well as galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) experiments in order to clarify the lithiation thermodynamics of tin (IV) sulfide. Based on the experimental data, a one-phase mechanism was suggested for the intercalation of lithium into SnS2, a thermodynamic model was developed to describe the intercalation reaction and the expected open circuit voltages were calculated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Crystal structure of SnS2 with Sn atoms (in red) located at the 1a octahedrally coordinated Wyckoff positions. The S atoms occupy the hexagonally packed 2d positions in the crystal lattice (in yellow). (b) Crystal structure of LiSnS2, with Li atoms (in blue) occupying the 1b inter-layer octahedra positions. (c) Crystal structure of LiSnS2 showing the intra-layer (dark grey) and the inter-layer (light grey) 2d tetrahedral positions. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The calculated ternary Li–S–Sn phase diagram at 298.15 K (centre) with the constituent S–Sn (left) and Li–Sn (right) binaries.

The calculated ternary Li–S–Sn phase diagram at 298.15 K with the equilibrium lithiation pathway of SnS2 represented as the solid grey line (left). The calculated emf values vs Li+/Li as well as the phase constitution on lithiation are shown on the right.

Measured, calculated, and difference PXRD patterns for the SnS2 powder sample from MKNano. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Scanning electron micrographs of the SnS2 powder samples from MKNano showing larger secondary particles (left) with platelet-like SnS2 primary particles (right).

Measured discharge curve of the SnS2 electrode active material (left) and the results of the ex-situ PXRD measurements of the electrode samples discharged to different capacities (right). In the PXRD measurements, the pentagon indicates reflections for SnS2, the circles indicate those for the Cu foil substrate, the star for the (1 1 1) reflection of Li2S, the inverted triangle for the (2 0 0) and (1 0 0) reflections of Sn, the triangle for the (0 0 1) reflection of Li2Sn5 and the square for the (8 2 2) reflection of Li17Sn4. The peak associated with the polycarbonate dome, which was used to perform the measurements in an Ar-atmosphere, is indicated with the diamond. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Galvanostatic intermittent titration techniques measurements for SnS2 vs Li+/Li on discharge up to 0.01 V and charge up to 2 V. A current rate of 30 mA/g for 30 min was used for the titration steps. The titration curve calculated using the thermodynamic description of the Li–S–Sn system is also shown. The value of x in LixSnS2 is determined using the measured specific capacity according to the equation x = 3.6 mw C/F where mw is the molar mass of SnS2, C is the measured capacity and F is Faraday's constant.

Results of the galvanostatic titration intermittent technique experiment for electrochemical lithiation of SnS2 up to the Li0.48SnS2 composition, followed by de-lithiation back to SnS2. These experiments were performed using a standard deviation of 0.05 mV for the quasi-equilibrium potentials instead of the usual 0.1 mV as in Fig. 7. For improved resolution, the titration currents of 30 mA/g were applied for 15 min instead of the usual 30 min. The value of x in LixSnS2 is determined using the measured specific capacity according to the equation x = 3.6 mw C/F where mw is the molar mass of SnS2, C is the measured capacity and F is Faraday's constant. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Measured, calculated, and difference patterns for the (top) SnS2 electrode, which was lithiated to Li0.48SnS2 in a lithium half-cell using galvanostatic titration and (bottom) SnS2 electrode, which was lithiated to Li0.48SnS2 and de-lithiated to SnS2 in a lithium half-cell using galvanostatic titration. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
CV curve of SnS2 electrode, which was discharged to 0.01 V and charged to 2 V vs Li+/Li in lithium half-cell with a scan rate of 0.03 mV/s.

Quasi-open circuit potentials as a function of the mole fraction of lithium in LixSnS2. The extrapolated partial Gibbs free energy for the intercalation of Li in SnS2 can be determined by extrapolating the data back to x = 0 and (dashed line) applying the equation µ(Li)= - zFE to the extrapolated potential at x = 0.

The agreement between the calculated and the experimentally determined quasi-open circuit voltages for the lithiation of SnS2 up to Li0.48SnS2. Since LixSnS2 is metastable, the quasi-open circuit voltages can only be calculated when all other tin sulfides are omitted (red line). When the other tin sulfides are included, a three-phase field Li0.022SnS2 + Sn2S3 + Li2S with a constant voltage of 2.12 V is calculated (blue line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Calculated titration curves for the full lithiation of SnS2 compared to the GITT measurements. The presence of the metastable LixSnS2 phase alters the calculated voltage profile (red line) compared to that for the equilibrium lithiation of SnS2 (grey line) up to completion of the conversion reaction at x = 4 in LixSnS2. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Isothermal section of the Li–S–Sn system calculated using the Gibbs free energy description of the metastable LixSnS2 phase. The intercalation of Li into the crystal structure of SnS2 occurs along the line SnS2–Li1.5SnS2. After intercalation is complete, the conversion reaction takes place, leading to the formation of the Li1.5SnS2 + Li2S + Sn three-phase field.

Results of the Rietveld refinement of the ex-situ XRD measurements performed on the as-received SnS2 powder sample as well as electrodes which were either cycled or subjected to the galvanostatic intermittent titration technique.

head.ce:title: Understanding and modelling the thermodynamics and electrochemistry of lithiation of tin (IV) sulfide as an anode active material for lithium ion batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tin (IV) sulfide is a promising anode active material for lithium ion batteries due to its relatively high reversible capacity of 644 mAh/g, which is more than one and a half times that of graphite. During lithiation of tin (IV) sulfide, an inert Li2S matrix is formed in the first discharge cycle, which serves to accommodate the mechanical stresses associated with the volume expansion of tin during the successive LixSn alloying and de-alloying reactions. In order to improve the electrochemical performance of tin (IV) sulfide further, fundamental understanding and insights into the thermodynamics, phase formation, and driving forces for the lithiation reactions are still required. Therefore, in this work, a computational thermodynamics approach was combined with ex-situ XRD investigations of electrodes during the discharge reaction as well as galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) experiments in order to clarify the lithiation thermodynamics of tin (IV) sulfide. Based on the experimental data, a one-phase mechanism was suggested for the intercalation of lithium into SnS2, a thermodynamic model was developed to describe the intercalation reaction and the expected open circuit voltages were calculated.

ce:section.ce:para: The chemistry, micro-structural morphology, and processing of existing cathode and anode active materials must continuously be optimized in order to develop lithium ion batteries (LIBs) with higher energy and power densities, fast charging capabilities, improved safety, and environmental sustainability at low production costs. Current trends in anode active materials for LIBs foresee the replacement of the graphite-based anode, which has a theoretical capacity of 372 mAh/g, with materials which exhibit significantly higher capacities. Tin (Sn) is considered an interesting anode active material as it is naturally abundant and has a theoretical capacity of 960 mAh/g, which is approximately two and a half times that of graphite. Additionally, since the operating voltage of alloy-based electrodes is ~1 to ~0.01 V vs Li+/Li, potential safety issues associated with the electrodeposition and dendritic growth of lithium during fast charging can be reduced [1]. However, Sn-based anodes, like all metal and semi-metal anodes, suffer from prohibitively large volume expansions due to the formation of various LixSn alloys during lithiation (>185% for full lithiation to Li4.4Sn). Since the LixSn intermetallics are brittle, the volume changes lead to extensive crack formation within the electrode active material [2]. As a result, several degradation mechanisms take place during cycling, including loss of electrical contact between the active material and the current collector, lithium trapping, aggregation and isolation of alloy particles, and the growth of an unstable solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) [3]. In addition, such large volume changes increase internal stresses on the other cell components, which is a particular issue for pouch cells, which can expand externally.

One way to take advantage of the high capacity of Sn and simultaneously mitigate the degradation mechanisms associated with its excessive volume expansion is to use SnS2 as an anode active material. SnS2 belongs to the family of Sn-based chalcogenide compounds, which also includes SnO2, SnS, SnSe and SnSe2, All of these materials have already been explored as anode materials for Li- and Na-ion batteries and review articles are available in [4–6].

SnS2 is an interesting anode material for lithium-ion batteries due to its relatively high reversible capacity of 644 mAh/g, which is almost double that of graphite. It exhibits a hexagonal, layered CdI2 structure (Space group P-3m1, No. 164) with edge-sharing S–Sn octahedra within the layers and S–Sn–S stacking across the layers along the crystallographic c-direction [0 0 1] (Fig. 1 , created using VESTA [7]) . The S atoms occupy 2d positions (z ~ 0.25) forming the hexagonally packed host lattice (yellow atoms in Fig. 1a) whereas the Sn atoms occupy the intra-layer 1a octahedral Wyckoff positions (red atoms in Fig. 1a).

The complete lithiation of SnS2 takes place via successive intercalation, conversion, and alloying reaction mechanisms. The intercalation of Li is enabled by: 1) the 4+ oxidation state of the Sn cation, which can be reduced to 2+, and 2) the presence of vacant inter-layer octahedral 1b positions (blue atoms in Fig. 1b and c) and inter- and intra-layer tetrahedral 2d positions with z ~ 0.57 and z ~ 0.93, respectively (light and dark grey atoms in Fig. 1c). Additionally, the tetrahedral sites are large enough to contribute to vacancy solid state diffusion of Li in the LixSnS2 structure.

According to Lefebvre-Devos et al. [8], during intercalation of lithium into SnS2, lithium ions are first inserted only into the inter-layer octahedral sites up to x = 0.16, whereas the inter-layer sites are the preferred intercalation sites up to x = 0.49. For compositions up to x = 1.66, a mixed occupation of the inter-layer octahedral and intra-layer tetrahedral sites is observed [8]. The intercalation reactions occur at potentials between 1.6 and 1.9 V vs Li+/Li [9,10,11] and can be formulated as:
(1) SnS2 + x∙Li+ + x∙e – → LixSnS2

After intercalation, lithiation proceeds via the conversion and alloying reactions, respectively. During the irreversible conversion reaction, which takes place at potentials between 1.0 and 1.1 V vs Li+/Li [9,10,11], a composite electrode microstructure consisting of Sn particles embedded in an inert Li2S matrix is produced according to the reaction:
(2) LixSnS2 + (4 – x)∙Li+ + (4 – x)∙e – → 2∙Li2S + Sn

At potentials lower than ~0.8 V vs Li+/Li, the reversible alloying and de-alloying reactions of the Sn particles can occur following the chemical reaction:
(3) Sn + y∙Li+ + y∙e – → LiySn

During the alloying and de-alloying reactions, the electrochemically inactive Li2S matrix should accommodate the mechanical stresses associated with the volume changes of Sn [12] and prevent aggregation of the active material particles to improve cyclic stability [13]. The theoretical capacity associated with the full lithiation of SnS2 is 1209 mAh/g, whereas that for the alloying reaction alone is 644 mAh/g.

In order to improve the cycle-to-cycle coulombic efficiency of the alloying reaction, two approaches are usually followed in the literature. The first approach involves reducing the particle sizes [14] and designing SnS2 structures with unique morphologies. SnS2 has thus been synthesized as nanosheets [9], nanoflakes [15], nanorods [16,17], and nanobelts [18]. Both the hydrothermal [13,19,20] and the solvothermal [21,22,23] methods have proven to be suitable for controlling the particles’ sizes and tuning their morphologies. Other routes which were used to synthesize SnS2 include the wet chemistry method [24], liquid-solid phase reactions [25], the isothermal [26] and sonochemical methods [27], as well as the polyol route [28]. In the second approach, SnS2/carbonaceous material composites are fabricated in order to increase the electrical conductivity of the electrode [29,30,31,32,33]. Such graphene and/or carbon-based networks are required since the Li2S matrix that forms during the conversion reaction has a low electronic conductivity, which in turn inhibits the introduction and extraction of electrons during lithium ion uptake and removal.

Several authors have proposed a solid-solution behaviour for the lithiation of SnS2. Morales et al. [34] performed chemical and electrochemical lithiation of SnS2 and showed that during lithiation, the LixSnS2 phase is isostructural with SnS2 at lithium contents of x < 0.6. However, at higher lithium concentrations, an intermediate phase with larger intra-layer spacing and Li ions located in trigonal prismatic coordinated positions was observed. In another work, Lefebvre-Devos et al. performed chemical lithiation of SnS2 and analysed the lithiated phases using 119Sn Mössbauer spectroscopy, X-ray absorption spectroscopy, and electronic structure calculations [8]. They showed that at lithium contents up to x = 0.16 in LixSnS2, lithium ions are inserted into the inter-layer octahedral positions, thereby inducing only small distortions of the host lattice. At higher lithium contents of 0.49 < x < 1.21, lithium ions are inserted into both the inter-layer octahedral positions as well as the intra-layer tetrahedral sites. This leads to larger lattice distortions and lower crystallinity, until the layered SnS2 structure ceases to exist at x = 1.66.

Although Lefebvre-Devos et al. [8], Morales et al. [34] indicate that SnS2 can accommodate lithium ions in its host structure, there are still many open questions regarding: 1) the amount of lithium which can be intercalated into the layered structure of SnS2, 2) the structural changes of SnS2 during lithiation, and 3) whether the reaction occurs via a one-phase or a two-phase mechanism. For example, Gao et al. tracked the insertion and extraction of lithium from SnS2 via in-situ high-resolution TEM [35] and showed that during the lithiation reaction, pristine SnS2 co-exists with SnS2 defect structures exhibiting antiphase boundaries and edge dislocations. However, the de-lithiation reaction occurs via a solid-solution mechanism, in which intermediate Li0.5SnS2 superstructures with 1–4 nm domain sizes are formed [35]. In a more recent work, Hwang et al. [36] explored the structural evolution of SnS2 during lithiation using in-situ electron diffraction microscopy and in-situ imaging. Based on their results, a two-phase mechanism was also proposed for the lithiation of SnS2 up to LiSnS2. In this composition range, SnS2 co-exists with a disordered rock-salt phase with LiSnS2 stoichiometry.

Phase diagrams are essential for understanding the phase transformations which can occur during lithiation and de-lithiation of electrode active materials. However, the only available phase diagram in the Li–S–Sn system is that of Hwang et al. [36], which was determined at 0 K using DFT calculations. Unfortunately, this phase diagram is incomplete since Hwang et al. omitted the Sn2S3, Li7Sn3 and Li5Sn2 phases and did not consider the electrochemically and chemically observed solubility of lithium in SnS2, as determined by Lefebvre-Devos et al. [8] and Morales et al. [34].

Since the electrochemistry and thermodynamics of the lithiation of SnS2 are still unclear, we chose to perform a thermodynamic modelling, simulation, and experimental approach based on the CALculation of PHAse Diagrams (CALPHAD) and computational thermodynamics methods [37] to:
1)
calculate the Li–S–Sn phase diagram at 298 K
2)
predict the compositional changes and phase transformations of the SnS2-based electrode materials during equilibrium lithiation,
3)
assess the differences between the equilibrium and non-equilibrium lithiation pathways of investigated electrode samples,
4)
suggest areas where the formation of metastable phases and/or partially amorphous states strongly influence the electrochemical reaction mechanism, and
5)
develop a new thermodynamic model for the lithiation of SnS2.

In the CALPHAD method, the Gibbs free energies of selected phases in a materials system are described as analytical functions of temperature, pressure, and composition. These expressions are developed based on semi-empirical thermodynamic models of the phases, which are specifically selected to match their crystallography. The adjustable thermodynamic parameters are optimized in a step-by-step procedure using all available experimental and theoretical data. In “computational thermodynamics”, the Gibbs energy functions are used for application-orientated simulations to explore specific material-property relationships [37].

In order to develop a description for the ternary Li–S–Sn system, the Gibbs free energy models for the binary S–Sn [38] and Li–Sn [39] systems were merged, and an analytic Gibbs free energy expression for Li2S, which is available in the SGTE substance database [40], was added. Since there are no data on the ternary extensions of the SnS, Sn2S3, Li2S, and Li–Sn compounds, they were all treated as purely binary phases. On the other hand, SnS2 appears to form a solid solution between SnS2 and Li2S. Kuhn et al. expressed this composition range as Li4xSn1-xS2, where 0 ≤ x ≤ 1 [41]. In that work, wet chemistry techniques were used to synthesize several compositions with 0 < x < 1/3 as well as the end-member with Li2SnS3 stoichiometry. Additionally, a Li4SnS4 compound (x = 0.5 in Li4xSn1-xS2), which is structurally related to the Li4GeS4 thio-LISICON phase, was synthesized by Kaib et al. [42]. Although these phases may be thermodynamically stable, there are no thermochemical and phase-diagram data available to model their Gibbs free energies with the CALPHAD method. Furthermore, since the Li4xSn1-xS2 solid solution exists between Li2S and SnS2 and not between Li and SnS2, none of these phases is expected to form during lithiation of SnS2, and therefore they were not considered in this work.

A commercially available SnS2 powder (MKNano, 99% Purity, average particle size ~ 10 µm) was obtained, characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM), fabricated into electrodes, and subjected to electrochemical testing vs Li+/Li counter electrodes in CR 2016 coin cell formats. Powder X-ray diffraction of the commercial tin sulfide sample was performed using a Bruker D8 diffractometer (Θ/2Θ-geometry) with a copper radiation source (40 kV / 40 mA) and a Ni-filter. Before the measurements, the powder was fixed onto a silicon mono-crystal sample holder using petroleum jelly. Signals were detected by a Lynxeye strip detector covering ±1.5 ° in 2Θ at the same time. The obtained patterns were evaluated based on Rietveld-refinements using the Topas4Ⓡ software. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed using an ESEM Zeiss Supra 55 VP instrument. The acceleration voltage for the electron beam was 5 kV and imaging was accomplished with a secondary electron detector. A thin layer of gold was sputtered onto the sample powder in order to avoid electron charge effects.

The electrochemical properties of the commercially available tin sulfide powder sample were investigated by the galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) and cyclic voltammetry (CV). The slurries for the electrode coatings were prepared using the SnS2 powder as the active material, carbon black Super C45 (Imerys) as the conductive additive, Solef 5130 polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, Solvay) as the binder, and n-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, Sigma Aldrich) as the organic solvent. For slurry preparation, the active material, conductive additive, and PVDF binder (dissolved in NMP) were mixed in a mass ratio of 80: 10: 10. The required masses of active material and conductive additive were weighed, mixed with each other, and homogenized in a mortar before adding the solution of PVDF dissolved in NMP. The as-prepared slurries were stirred overnight at room temperature using a magnetic stirrer and coated onto copper foil via tape casting with a doctor blade of 60 µm gap height. The coated electrodes were subsequently dried under vacuum at 80 °C for 12 h, cut into discs with a diameter of 15 mm using a heavy-duty disc cutter, and dried again in a flask under vacuum at 120 °C for 5 h. After the second drying step, the flask with the electrodes was sealed, disconnected from the vacuum, and directly introduced into an Ar-filled glove box, in which the H2O and O2 contents are in the ppm range. The active material mass loading of the 15 mm diameter electrodes is 1.907±0.023 mg/cm2 (sample size n = 30; the expanded uncertainty shown corresponds to a 95% confidence interval).

All coin cells were constructed in the glove box using a 1 M solution of LiPF6 in 3:7 wt mixture of ethylene carbonate and ethyl methyl carbonate with 2 wt% of vinylene carbonate additive (Soulbrain) as the electrolyte. Lithium discs (PI-KEM, 99.9% purity, thickness 0.6 mm) were used as the counter electrodes, whereas Celgard 2500 (polypropylene film, thickness 0.25 µm) was used as the separator.

In order to fully characterize the phase changes of the SnS2 electrode active material during discharge, ex-situ XRD was performed on cells which were discharged using a constant specific current of 20 mA/g to different end-capacities (C = 100•x mAh/g, where C is the end capacity and x = 0,1,2,…12). After discharge, the cells were opened in an Ar-filled glove box and the electrodes extracted and washed three times in dimethyl carbonate solvent. After drying, the electrodes were fixed onto silicon mono-crystal specimen holders and covered by airtight polycarbonate domes inside the Ar-filled glove box and then transferred to the diffractometer. Rietveld full-profile refinement of the measured XRD patterns of the electrodes was performed with Topas4Ⓡ software.

The galvanostatic intermittent titration technique was performed in order to characterize the electrochemical behaviour of the tin sulfides vs Li+/Li under quasi-equilibrium conditions. For the GITT measurements, a titration current of 30 mA/g was applied to the cells for a time interval of 30 min and then followed by a relaxation step. During this step, the voltage was measured once each minute for 10 min, after which the standard deviation was calculated using the ten obtained data points. If the standard deviation was less than 0.1 mV, the cell proceeded to the next titration step. However, if the standard deviation was larger than 0.1 mV, the relaxation step continued, the voltages were measured for an additional 10 min, and the standard deviation was checked once again. This step continued in a loop until a standard deviation < 0. 1 mV was obtained. This algorithm resulted in relaxation times for each step ranging from up to 30 h on discharge to 10 h on charge. The GITT measurements were performed in discharge up to 0.01 V vs Li+/Li and in charge up to various end potentials. In order to initialize the electrodes for the GITT measurements on charge, the half cells were discharged to 0.01 V using a discharge current of 30 mA/g, followed by a constant voltage step until a limiting current of 3 mA/g.

Finally, CV measurements were performed on SnS2 electrodes with a scan rate of 0.03 mV/s in the potential range of 0.1 and 2.0 V vs. Li/Li+. The CV measurements were conducted to correlate the potentials of the current peaks with the phase evolution of the tin sulfide active electrode material as determined via the GITT and ex-situ XRD measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Equilibrium phase diagram and lithiation of SnS2
ce:section.ce:para: All thermodynamic simulations presented in this section were performed using the POLY3 module of the software Thermo-Calc [43]. The calculated equilibrium Li–S–Sn phase diagram at 298.15 K and its constituent binaries are shown in Fig. 2 . This isothermal section can already be considered a reasonably accurate estimation of the known phase equilibria in the Li–S–Sn system at room temperature, since it is calculated using self-consistent thermodynamic descriptions of the respective binaries. However, one limitation is that the solid solution from SnS2 to Li2S was not modelled at this point due to the lack of thermodynamic data.

As defined by the thermodynamic models, the calculated isothermal section at 298.15 K shows no ternary solubilities of the Li–Sn alloys, Li2S, SnS2, Sn2S3 and SnS. Since a direct equilibrium exists between the pure component Sn and the binary compound Li2S, the Li–S–Sn system is split into two partial Li2S–S–Sn and Li–Li2S–Sn systems. The Li2S–S–Sn system is dominated by four three-phase equilibria, which are formed between the stable tin sulfides, the elements Sn or S, and Li2S. Similarly, the Li–Li2S–Sn partial system exhibits eight three-phase equilibria, which form between the respective binary Li–Sn intermetallic phases, the elements Sn or Li, and Li2S. Since Li2S is so thermodynamically stable, it is involved in all three-phase equilibria in the system. Consequently, adding a third component to any of the binary compounds will lead to the concomitant precipitation of a neighbouring phase in the binary system and Li2S. For example, adding Li to SnS will lead to the formation of Li2S and Sn, whereas adding S to LiSn will lead to the formation of Li2S and Li2Sn5.

Using this dataset, the lithiation of the various tin sulfides including their lithiation pathways, phase development, and open circuit voltages were calculated. These are illustrated for SnS2 in Fig. 3 , whereas those for SnS and Sn2S3 are shown in the supplementary information. The results of these thermodynamic calculations will be subsequently designated throughout the text as the “equilibrium lithiation” of the tin-sulfides.

The measured equilibrium potential (emf) in a half-cell setup is equal to -µLi⋅F, where µLi is the chemical potential of Li in the working electrode and F is the Faraday-constant. In a ternary system, the respective chemical potentials µi of the components are equal in all constituent phases of a three-phase equilibrium. Therefore, the calculated potential vs concentration curve on lithiation shows a sequence of plateaus, which corresponds to the sequence of three-phase equilibria, see Fig. 3. According to the calculations, the first stage in the equilibrium lithiation of SnS2 is characterized by the formation of Sn2S3 and Li2S in a conversion-type reaction mechanism at a potential of 2.12 V vs Li+/Li. In this reaction, which is given in Eq. (4) below, one half of the Sn4+ cations are reduced to Sn2+, resulting in a discharge capacity of 146.6 mAh/g.
(4) SnS2 + Li+ + e – → 1/2∙Li2S + 1/2∙Sn2S3

Once all SnS2 is consumed, lithiation continues via the reduction of the Sn4+ ions in Sn2S3 to Sn2+, thereby resulting in the formation of orthorhombic SnS and additional Li2S at a plateau potential of 2.05 V vs Li+/Li. This second conversion reaction can be written as:
(5) 1/2∙Sn2S3 + Li+ + e – → 1/2∙Li2S + SnS

Since one Sn4+ cation is reduced to Sn2+ per formula unit of the original SnS2 phase, the second conversion reaction also has a capacity of 146.6 mAh/g. Summing up the two reactions, a total capacity of 293.2 mAh/g for the formation of SnS from SnS2 and Li is obtained. Once the second reaction is complete, further lithiation results in the reduction of the Sn2+ ions to Sn0 at a plateau potential of 1.71 V vs Li+/Li according to the reaction:
(6) SnS + 2∙Li+ + 2∙e – → Li2S + Sn

Based on all three reactions, the equilibrium conversion of SnS2 to Sn and Li2S is associated with the uptake of four lithium ions and electrons per formula unit of SnS2. After the conversion reaction, the continued lithiation is dominated by the alloying of Sn and Li to form various LixSn intermetallic phases up to the Li17Sn4 composition. The successive alloying reactions are characterized by potential plateaus in the calculated titration curves, during which the chemical potential of lithium remains constant. Since Li2S is stable, it does not participate in any of these reactions but co-exists in equilibrium with the LixSn compounds. The values of all calculated plateau potentials can be found in the supplementary information.

The measured and calculated PXRD patterns of the SnS2 powder sample as well as the difference curve are shown in Fig. 4 , whereas the results of the Rietveld refinement are presented in Table 1 (see sample “SnS2 powder”). The as-received SnS2 powder is phase pure with preferred orientations in the edge [0 0 1] and face-diagonal [0 1 1] directions, which are in accordance with the layered hexagonal symmetry of SnS2. Preferred growth orientations are also evident in the SEM micrographs of SnS2 (Fig. 5 ), which show larger secondary particles with platelet-like SnS2 primary particles.

In order to explore the phase evolution of the active electrode materials during discharge, ex-situ XRD was performed on the pristine electrode and on those which were discharged to various end-capacities (C = 100•x mAh/g, where C is the end capacity and x = 0,1,2,…12). The discharge curve of the SnS2 active material measured at a specific current of 20 mA/g and the XRD patterns of the pristine and 12 discharged electrodes are shown in Fig. 6 , whereas the detailed results of the Rietveld refinements are given in Table 2 . The measured, calculated, and difference XRD patterns for each of the electrodes investigated in this study can be found in the supplementary information. In all XRD patterns of the electrode samples, the large diffuse peak at 2θ approx. 20° originates from the polycarbonate dome, which was used to perform the XRD measurements in an inert atmosphere. Furthermore, the peaks at 2θ = 43.3, 50.4, 74.1, 89.9, 95.1 and 117.0° are associated with the (1 1 1), (0 0 2), (0 2 2), (3 1 1), (2 2 2) and (0 0 4) reflections of the Cu foil substrate.

Although the SnS2 electrode sample is phase pure, the small peak at 2θ = 13.6°, corresponding to a d-spacing = 6.49 Å, may indicate the presence of a superstructure originating from stacking faults along the c-direction. This can be expected for layered structured materials. As the extent of lithiation increases between 100 mAh/g and 300 mAh/g, only SnS2 is observed with no significant changes in its c-parameter (5.897±0.002 Å at 100 mAh/g compared with 5.85±0.06 Å at 300 mAh/g). However, additional low-intensity reflexes for another superstructure occur at 2θ = 14.1, 15.9, 28.6, 33.2, 47.6 and 59.0°, which correspond to d-spacings of 6.28, 5.57, 3.12, 3.00, 1.91 and 1.56 Å respectively. The additional reflections may be a result of Li occupying intralayer tetrahedral positions in the SnS2 lattice at the LixSnS2 compositions, where x = 0.68 at 100 mAh/g, x = 1.36 at 200 mAh/g and x = 2.05 at 300 mAh/g. This mechanism was confirmed by Pietrass et al. [44], who used 6.7Li and 119Sn solid state NMR to show that at x > 0.5, insertion of Li into the interlayer sites is “forbidden” and only the intralayer sites are active. According to our XRD results, the additional reflexes become broader and their intensities decrease as the Li content of SnS2 increases. This could be coincident with a rupturing of the SnS2 layers and increasing amorphization, which was also observed by Pietrass et al. as the SnS2 structure starts to decompose into Li2S.

At a capacity of 300 mAh/g, the (1 1 1) reflection, which is the strongest for Li2S, appears at 2θ = 26.8° As the conversion reaction to Li2S and Sn proceeds, the intensity of the (1 1 1) reflection increases as more Li2S is formed. At 400 mAh/g, Sn is observed for the first time, along with unreacted SnS2, which could still be identified due to its strong preferred orientation along the [0 0 1] crystallographic direction. The presence of Li2S and Sn at 400 mAh/g thus confirms the conversion reaction of SnS2 during lithiation. The capacity of 600 mAh/g appears to be a turning point in the conversion reaction, as at that capacity, the intensities of the (2 0 0) and (1 0 0) reflections for Sn at 2θ = 30.67 and 32.06°, respectively reach their maximum. Simultaneously, this is the last step at which residual, non-reacted SnS2 could be observed via its (0 0 1) reflection.

The beginning of the alloying reaction of Sn and Li can be observed in the XRD pattern of the electrode discharged to a capacity of 700 mAh/g. The intensities of the (2 0 0) and (1 0 0) reflections for Sn have decreased, whereas the (0 0 1) reflection for Li2Sn5 appears at 2θ =26.9° It must be noted that since the (0 0 1) reflection of Li2Sn5 overlaps with the (1 1 1) of Li2S, the two phases can only be distinguished from each other via a full-profile Rietveld refinement of the measured XRD pattern. At a capacity of 800 mAh/g, Sn could no longer be identified as a crystalline phase in the electrode sample and is therefore most probably amorphous. As the alloying reaction proceeds beyond 800 mAh/g, Li2Sn5 is the only intermetallic phase observed until a capacity of 1000 mAh/g, at which the (8 2 2) reflection of Li17Sn4 appears at 2θ = 38.4° for the first time. At the end of discharge, corresponding to a potential of 0.01 mAh/g vs Li+/Li and a capacity of 1211 mAh/g, only Li2S and Li17Sn4 could be observed in the electrode active material.

The GITT measurement of SnS2 vs Li+/Li for discharge to 0.01 V followed by charge to 2.0 V is shown in Fig. 7 . In the GITT measurement, a total discharge capacity of 1224 mAh/g was obtained, which is nominally larger than the theoretical capacity of 1209 mAh/g expected for full lithiation of SnS2 to Li2S and Li17Sn4.

During discharge under GITT conditions, four distinct regions are observed in the measured curve. Using LixSnS2 notation based on a theoretical discharge capacity of 1209 mAh/g, the four regions can be expressed as: I) lithiation to Li0.6SnS2; II) lithiation between Li0.6SnS2 and Li1.5SnS2; III) lithiation between Li1.5SnS2 and Li3.7SnS2; and IV) lithiation between Li3.7SnS2 and Li8.3SnS2. However, during charge up to 2.0 V vs Li+/Li, only a single region from Li8.3SnS2 to Li3.6SnS2 is measured. In order to check the phase constitution of the active material after charge, ex-situ XRD was performed on an electrode which was discharged to 0.01 V and then charged to 2.0 V vs Li+/Li, both under galvanostatic titration conditions. According to the Rietveld refinement of the measured XRD pattern (see sample “SnS2 Electrode 1–1″ in Table 1 and the measured, calculated and difference curves in Supplementary Information), only Li2S and pure Sn phases are observed, confirming that the Li17Sn4 phase has de-alloyed to produce pure Sn.

The sloped shapes of the GITT curve in region I up to x = 0.6 and in region II up to x = 1.5 indicate that lithiation of SnS2 up to x = 1.5 occurs via a single-phase mechanism. This conclusion is in good agreement with the work of Lefebvre-Devos et al. [8] and Morales et al. [34]. However, Gao et al. [35] and Hwang et al. [36] propose a two-phase mechanism for the initial lithiation of SnS2, in which lithiated and non-lithiated phases are separated from each other by a defined phase boundary. In such a case, a flat voltage profile for the GITT curve would be expected. It must be mentioned, however, that the conclusions of Gao et al. [35] and Hwang et al. [36] were drawn based on the results of continuous lithiation experiments in which no equilibration steps were performed.

In order to explore these aspects further, more precise galvanostatic titration experiments were performed in a limited composition range for discharge up to Li0.48SnS2 and charge back to SnS2. Li0.48SnS2 was selected as the end composition for these experiments in order to avoid mixed interstitial site occupation, as Lefebvre-Devos et al. [8] and Pietrass et al. [44] show that starting at x>0.49, not only the inter-layer octahedral, but also the intra-layer tetrahedral sites can be occupied by lithium ions, and the XRD experiments of our discharged electrodes between Li0.68SnS2 and Li2.05SnS2 confirmed these results. For the more precise titration experiments, a standard deviation of 0.05 mV in the voltage was selected as the condition for quasi-equilibrium instead of the usual 0.1 mV for all previous experiments. Additionally, the titration currents of 30 mA/g were applied for 15 min instead of 30 min, therefore achieving better concentration-dependant resolution of the quasi-equilibrium potentials.

The GITT measurement for the partial lithiation and de-lithiation of SnS2 is shown in Fig. 8 . As already seen in the previous experiments, the profile of the quasi-open circuit voltages during lithiation is sloped. Interestingly, however, the potentials during constant current flux are flat from Li0.2SnS2 to Li0.48SnS2, as shown by the red dashed line in Fig. 8. The ex-situ XRD pattern of the electrode discharged to Li0.48SnS2 in a lithium half-cell under the same galvanostatic titration conditions is shown in Fig. 9 and the results of the Rietveld analysis are given in Table 1 (see sample “SnS2 Electrode 1–16″). As expected, only the SnS2 phase could be observed, with no changes in the a and c parameters within the error of the Rietveld refinement.

Usually, the intercalation of Li+ into host structures is accompanied by an increase in lattice parameters. In the case of e.g.TiS2, which crystallizes in the same structure type (CdI2) as SnS2, the lattice parameters continuously change on lithiation from a = 3.4073 Å, c = 5.6953 Å for TiS2 [45] to a = 3.459 Å, c = 6.1879 Å [46] for the fully lithiated compound LiTiS2. The intercalation of Li in between the S–Ti–S layers which are stacked along [0 0 1] explain the much larger increase of c compared to a. Morales et al. [34] proved the presence of Li in LixSnS2 via solvent co-intercalation experiments and measured the lattice parameters of the intercalated and partially de-intercalated LixSnS2 phases. They also reported only minor changes in the unit cell parameters and attributed this effect to the counterbalancing between the net attractive and repulsive forces between the layers [34]. Overall, there are various electrostatic, electron correlation, and polarization contributions which determine lattice spacing and structure of intercalation compounds. Specifically, during reduction of Sn4+ to Sn2+, which occurs in parallel with lithium intercalation, the S–Sn–S layers consisting of edge-sharing Sn-S6 octahedra adopt net negative charges and repel each other. On the other hand, these repulsive forces are counteracted by the coulombic attraction to the positively charged intercalated lithium ions located between the layers, resulting in no significant changes in the lattice parameters.

During the charge step of the galvanostatic titration measurement, significant over-potentials are observed between the constant current flux and quasi-open circuit potentials. These over-potentials become larger as the extent of de-lithiation increases. However, the plateau voltages at approximately 3.6 V vs Li+/Li during constant current flux are most likely due to corrosion of the copper current collector, which was shown by Zhao et al. to occur at 3.5 V vs Li+/Li as a consequence of copper dissolution into organic LiPF6-containing electrolytes [47]. To check the structure of the active electrode material after charge, ex-situ XRD was performed on an electrode which was lithiated under galvanostatic titration to Li0.48SnS2 and de-lithiated back to the SnS2 composition under galvanostatic titration (Fig. 9). The result of the Rietveld refinement of this electrode is given in Table 1 (see sample “SnS2 Electrode 1–15″). There is no observable change in the structure of the SnS2 phase, strongly indicating that both lithium insertion and extraction likely occur via a single-phase mechanism.

A single-phase, solid solution behaviour for the equilibrium state of lithiated SnS2 can be suggested based on: 1) the structural analysis of the Li0.48SnS2 composition showing the presence of a single phase with no change in lattice parameters relative to pure SnS2 and 2) the sloped profile of the quasi-open circuit voltages on discharge. This conclusion is in accordance with those of Lefebvre-Devos et al. [8] and Morales et al. [34]. On the other hand, the flat voltage profile observed at the constant current flux steps before equilibration shows that two phases are present during active lithiation of SnS2. This would be in agreement with the works of Gao et al. [35] and Hwang et al. [36]. Therefore, based on our experiments, the following mechanism is proposed: At the constant current step, the change in chemical potential of lithium drives the lithiation reaction of SnS2. This thermodynamic driving force is proportional to the measured over-potential in the titration step and is partially required for the formation of the phase boundary between the lithium-rich and lithium-poor regions of SnS2. As soon as the external driving force is removed in the equilibration step, the difference in chemical potential of lithium across the phase boundary generates a driving force for the diffusion of lithium to produce a continuous solid solution. Therefore, our GITT experiments could help clarify the seemingly contradictory conclusions of Lefebvre-Devos et al. [8] and Morales et al. [34], who propose a single-phase lithiation process based primarily on chemical lithiation experiments, with those of Gao et al. [35] and Hwang et al. [36], who suggest a two-phase mechanism based on continuous lithiation.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was also performed to confirm the electrochemical behaviour of the SnS2 electrode during the 1st lithiation and delithiation cycles (see Fig. 10 ). The CV curve shows two obvious cathodic peaks at 1.80 V and 1.18 V which can be attributed to the intercalation of lithium into the SnS2 layers and decomposition of the SnS2 into metallic Sn and Li2S as well as the formation of SEI. In fact, the onset potential of 1.37 V for the formation of Li2S and Sn in the CV curve fits well to the potential of 1.35 V at constant current flux for the same reaction in the GITT measurements (see Fig. 7). The reducing current below 0.5 V vs. Li+/Li could be assigned to the Li alloying and the formation of Li2Sn5 and Li17Sn4, which are confirmed by our post-mortem XRD measurements of the discharged electrodes (see Fig. 6 and discussion). At the anodic parts, three obvious peaks at 0.5, 0.6, and 1.7 V vs. Li+/Li are shown. The peak at 0.5 V vs. Li+/Li can be attributed to dealloying of Li17Sn4, while the peak at 1.7 V vs. Li/Li+could be attributed to the partial decomposition of Li2S. These are in good agreement with our previous observations as well as with the study reported by Zhang et al. [48].

where z is the number of moles of electrons transferred in the electrochemical reaction, F is the Faraday constant, and E(x) defines the equilibrium potential of the titration curve as a function of composition. Consequently, the Gibbs free energy of formation of a given phase constitution of the electrode is proportional to the total area under the equilibrium E–x titration curve, and the GITT measurements can be used to make qualitative assessments about the energetics of the lithiation reactions.

In Fig. 7, the quasi-open circuit voltages for the SnS2 electrode active material vs Li+/Li during formation of LixSnS2 up to x = 1.5 are always lower than the potentials calculated for equilibrium lithiation using the thermodynamic description of the Li–S–Sn system. Accordingly, the Gibbs free energy of formation of the lithiated LixSnS2 compositions is less negative than that for the formation of the stable tin sulfides and Li2S. Therefore, the intercalated LixSnS2 compositions can be considered as metastable, since they do not represent the lowest Gibbs energy configurations of the Li–S–Sn system at their respective compositions. As the degree of lithiation increases from x = 0 to 1.5, the difference in the integrated areas of the calculated and measured quasi-equilibrium open circuit voltages increases, indicating that the phase constitution of the electrode becomes less stable as the lithiation reaction proceeds.

In Fig. 8, the quasi-open circuit voltages measured during electrochemical lithiation of SnS2 to Li0.48SnS2 are also compared to the calculated open circuit voltages from the thermodynamic modelling (dashed black line). As mentioned before, these measurements were performed with smaller titration steps and more stringent criteria for the selection of the quasi-open circuit voltage. Therefore, these measurements are expected to track the equilibrium lithiation of the SnS2 phase better and to be useful to extract quantitative thermodynamic data on the lithiation of SnS2 such as the extrapolated partial Gibbs free energies. The extrapolated partial Gibbs free energy for the intercalation of lithium into SnS2 can be understood as the change in Gibbs free energy of the system when 1 mol of lithium is intercalated into an infinitely large amount of SnS2, in which a majority of the interstitial sites are unoccupied. The extrapolated partial Gibbs free energy (not the limited, which is infinity) can be determined from the data in Fig. 8 by plotting the quasi-open circuit potentials as a function of the mole fraction of lithium in LixSnS2, extrapolating the data back to x = 0 and applying the equation µ(Li)= - zFE to the extrapolated potential at x = 0 (see Fig. 11 ). Using this approach, a value of −202.3 kJ/mol was obtained. This value can be compared to the chemical potential of lithium in the SnS2 + Sn2S3 + Li2S three- phase field, which is the first phase constitution calculated to form during equilibrium lithiation of SnS2. Using the thermodynamic description developed in this work, a value of −204.6 kJ/mol is obtained, which is in good agreement with the extrapolated partial Gibbs free energy for the intercalation of lithium into SnS2. This indicates that at the start of the lithiation process, the energetics of the intercalation and Sn2S3 + Li2S formation reactions are indeed similar to each other. However, perhaps due to the ease of intercalation of lithium ions into the vacant octahedral and tetrahedral sites of SnS2, the metastable LixSnS2 phase is stabilized up to x = 1.5.

At compositions 1.5 < x < 4, lithium reacts with LixSnS2 to produce Li2S and Sn in a conversion type mechanism according to the reaction:
(8) LixSnS2 + (4 – x)∙Li+ + (4 – x)∙e – → 2∙Li2S + Sn

From a phase diagram point of view, this reaction is associated with the formation of a LixSnS2 + Li2S + Sn three-phase field, in which the composition of the LixSnS2 phase remains fixed throughout. In fact, this three-phase field was confirmed in the PXRD patterns of the electrodes discharged to 400, 500 and 600 mAh/g (see Table 2). Since the chemical potential of lithium in a three-phase field of a ternary system is constant at constant temperature and pressure, a voltage plateau should be recorded at 1.5 < x < 4. In our GITT measurements, however, a voltage plateau is only observed during constant current flux but not in the measured quasi-open circuit voltages after relaxation. This indicates that the conversion reaction takes place during constant current flux conditions, where the over-potential supplies enough energy to drive the nucleation and growth of Li2S and Sn domains. However, once this driving-force is removed, some LixSnS2 partially re-forms in the equilibration step. As the lithium contents of the re-formed LixSnS2 regions may be expected to increase as the total lithium content of the electrode increases, the quasi-open circuit voltages decrease with each titration step for values of x between 1.5 and 4.

Between x = 4 and 8.3 in Fig. 7, a voltage hysteresis but no distinct plateaus are observed for the several alloying and de-alloying reactions. In our work, the calculated potential plateaus for the equilibrium alloying reactions are located within the measured hysteresis for alloying (discharge) and de-alloying (charge) between x = 6.33 and 8.3. According to an analysis of the GITT method presented by Zhu et al. [49], such a voltage hysteresis is expected for phase transformation electrodes, in which the transitions are accompanied by large volume changes. The measured quasi-open circuit voltages are lower than the calculated (thermodynamic equilibrium) ones during alloying due to the elastic strain energies associated with the volume changes of the LixSn compounds, which form on lithiation. Applying the formalism given in Eq. (7), the measured potential is related to the equilibrium (calculated) potential and strain energy as:

Both Hirai et al. [50] and Zhang et al. [51] estimated the potential drops which occur during the lithiation of Sn. Using the elastic constants of β-Sn and an eigenstrain constant of 0.0688 for the formation of Li2Sn5 inclusions within a β-Sn particle, Hirai et al. calculated a potential drop from 0.77 to 0.528 V for Li2Sn5 formation from Sn, which fits well with their experimental open-circuit voltage measurements. It should be mentioned that the co-existence of Sn and Li2Sn5 was confirmed in our PXRD measurement of the electrode discharged to a specific capacity of 700mAh/g. Zhang et al. [51] used first principles calculations to determine the eigenstrains, stresses, elastic-strain energies, equilibrium open circuit voltages and voltage drops caused by the volume changes associated with the successive alloying reactions between Sn and Li. Their calculations accurately predict the potential drops for the first two plateaus, which result from the elastic-strain energies for the formation of Li2Sn5 and LiSn, respectively. Furthermore, their calculations show that the potential drop associated with the formation of Li7Sn2 from LiSn fits well to the experimental data if Li7Sn2 is allowed to adopt a distorted crystal lattice with smaller cell volumes to decrease the large elastic-strain energy. This correlates well with the work of Mukhopadhyay et al. [52], who observed enhanced stress release when LiSn is transformed to Li7Sn2 in Sn-based thin film electrodes.

In addition to calculating the extrapolated partial Gibbs free energy of intercalation of lithium into SnS2, the quasi-open circuit voltages for the lithiation of SnS2 up to Li0.48SnS2 were used as input data to develop a new thermodynamic model for the lithiated SnS2 phase. In this case, a three sublattice model (Li+, Va)1.5(Sn)1(S)2 was selected to describe the Gibbs free energy of LixSnS2. This is a simplified model, in which the first sublattice collectively represents the inter-layer 1b octahedral and intra-layer 2d tetrahedral sites as detailed by Lefebvre-Devos et al. [8], and the remaining two sublattices describe the 1a and 2d Wyckoff positions of the CdI2 prototype structure (Number 164), respectively. In non-lithiated SnS2, the 1b octahedral and 2d tetrahedral sites are not occupied and thus populated by vacancies (Va) only. However, as lithium is intercalated into the structure, the lithium ions and vacancies mix with each other on the interstitial sites. A stoichiometric factor of 1.5 was selected for the first sublattice in order to fix the maximum solubility of lithium in the SnS2 phase as observed by our GITT experiments. Since the chemical potential of the vacancies is zero, the Gibbs free energies of the two end-members, which are formed when the first sublattice is occupied by either lithium or vacancies only, are defined as:
(10)
G Va:Sn:S = G SnS2 = Gm (SnS2)
(11)
G Li:Sn:S = G Li1.5SnS2 = Gm (SnS2) + v1

In these expressions, G Va:Sn:
S = G SnS2 is the molar Gibbs free energy of pure SnS2, G Li:Sn:S is the Gibbs free energy of Li1.5SnS2, and v1 is the thermodynamic variable to be optimized. The mixing of lithium and vacancies on the first sublattice was described using a regular solution model as:
(12)
G Li,Va:Sn: S = v2
where v2 is the thermodynamic variable to be optimized. Since the GITT measurements were performed only at room temperature, any temperature dependences of the G Li,Va:Sn:S parameter could not be modelled. On the other hand, since the molar Gibbs free energy of SnS2 is used for the G Li:Sn:S parameter, the temperature dependence of the Li1.5SnS2 composition is the same as that for non-lithiated SnS2. The optimization of the thermodynamic parameters was performed using a least-squares algorithm in the PARROT module of the Thermo-Calc software [43]. The values of the parameters are given in the supplementary information.

In Fig. 12 , the agreement between the calculated and the experimentally determined quasi-open circuit voltages for the lithiation of SnS2 up to Li0.48SnS2 is shown. The new thermodynamic description of the lithiated SnS2 phase reproduces the experimentally determined quasi-open circuit voltage data up to x = 0.48 in LixSnS2 well. Since the lithiated SnS2 phase is metastable, however, the calculations can only be performed when the formation of the stable Sn2S3 and SnS phases is suppressed. When the stable phases are included, lithiation of SnS2 is calculated to occur up to x = 0.022, after which the three-phase field Li0.022SnS2 + Sn2S3 + Li2S is formed with a potential of 2.12 V (blue line in Fig. 12).

The calculated titration curve for the full lithiation of SnS2 is shown with the complete GITT measurements in Fig. 13 . As mentioned previously, since these GITT measurements were performed using less stringent criteria for the establishment of the quasi-open circuit voltage and larger titration steps, the measured quasi-open circuit potentials are not expected to reflect the true values accurately. In fact, as can be seen in Fig. 13, the calculated potentials for the lithiation of SnS2 up to Li0.48SnS2 are higher than the measured quasi-open circuit voltages, even though they are in good agreement with the quasi-open circuit voltages measured with smaller titration steps and more stringent quasi-equilibrium criteria (Fig. 12). Regardless of this discrepancy, important information with respect to the lithiation of SnS2 can still be extracted from the comparison of the data. For example, as the extent of lithiation of the SnS2 phase increases up to Li1.5SnS2, the deviation between the calculated and the experimentally determined titration curves increases. This could be a deficiency of the simplified thermodynamic model of the LixSnS2 phase, since no distinction was made between the inter-layer octahedral and intra-layer tetrahedral sites for lithium occupation.

An interesting phenomenon which occurs is that the calculated voltage plateau of 1.82 V for the three phase equilibria Li1.5SnS + Sn + Li2S is higher than the calculated 1.71 V plateau for the SnS + Sn + Li2S three- phase field. This can be explained as follows.

For the electrochemical process from x = 0 to 4, the total chemical reaction can be written as:
(13) SnS2 + 4∙Li → 2∙Li2S + Sn

According to the thermodynamic descriptions for the phases, the Gibbs free energy for this reaction at 298.15 K is –732.1 kJ. (For data on the Gibbs free energies of the individual phases used for the calculation, please refer to the supplementary information). Using Eq. (7), the total integral area of the potential–composition curve between x = 0 and x = 4 must be 7.59 J/As. Since the Gibbs free energy is a state function, the total integral area of the potential–composition curve depends only on the initial (SnS2 + Li at x = 0) and final (Li2S + Sn at x = 4) states of the system and is independent of the reaction pathway. As the area under the calculated composition–potential curve associated with the formation of the lithiated SnS2 phase is lower than that for the step-wise formation of Sn2S3 and SnS between x = 0 and x = 2, it necessarily means that the area under the potential–composition curve between x = 2 and x = 4 for the formation of Li2S + Sn from Li1.5SnS has to be higher than that for the formation of Li2S and Sn from SnS in order to yield a total area of 7.59 J/As. Therefore, the potential plateau associated with the lithiation of the intermediate Li1.5SnS2 phase to Li2S and Sn is higher than that for the lithiation of the stable SnS phase to Li2S and Sn.

The isothermal section for the lithiation of SnS2 via the formation of the lithiated LixSnS2 phase at 298.15 K was calculated using the thermodynamic description of the Li–S–Sn system and the new model for LixSnS2 (Fig. 14 ). Since the lithiated SnS2 phase is less energetically stable than the competing SnS2+Sn2S3+Li2S and Sn2S3+SnS+Li2S phase assemblages, the isothermal section can only be calculated when the formation of Sn2S3, SnS, and even the liquid phase is suppressed. As expected, due to the direct equilibrium between Li2S and Sn, the calculated isothermal section is divided into the Li–Li2S–Sn and Li2S–S–Sn partial sections. While the Li–Li2S–Sn section is unaffected and remains exactly as shown in Fig. 2, the Li2S–S–Sn section is different. The lithiated SnS2 phase now extends into the ternary system up to the Li1.5SnS2 composition in accordance with the LixSnS2 stoichiometry. Its maximum lithium solubility is x = 1.5, as observed in our GITT experiments (Fig. 7) and defined by the thermodynamic model. Two two-phase fields appear on either side of the lithiated SnS2 phase, i.e., LixSnS2 + Sn and LixSnS2 + Li2S, showing that any additions of Sn or S to LixSnS2 would result in the precipitation of free Sn and Li2S, respectively. In addition to the already existing SnS2 + Li2S + S three- phase field, a new three- phase field is calculated for the Li1.5SnS2 + Sn + Li2S equilibrium. In this field, the fully lithiated SnS2 phase with Li1.5SnS2 stoichiometry decomposes to Li2S and pure Sn as expected for the conversion mechanism. When the total composition of the electrode intersects the Li2S + Sn direct equilibrium line, all Li1.5SnS2 has decomposed and only Li2S and Sn exist in the electrode. After this point, lithiation continues via formation of the LixSn alloys, which co-exist with the stable Li2S phase.

In this work, a thermodynamic description of the Li–S–Sn system at 298 K was developed in order to calculate the phase equilibria and open circuit voltages as a function of lithium content for the equilibrium lithiation of tin (IV) sulfide. Additionally, ex-situ XRD investigations of electrodes at various specific capcities along the discharge curve as well as the galvanostatic intermittent titration technique were used to gain further insights into the thermodynamics and mechanisms of the lithiation reactions. Using these data, a new thermodynamic model could be developed for the Gibbs free energy of SnS2 to describe the intercalation of lithium ions into the SnS2 crystal lattice and calculate the expected voltage profile.

According to our combined computational thermodynamics and experimental approach, lithiated LixSnS2 is a metastable phase which forms a continuous solid solution from SnS2 to Li1.5SnS2. The solid solution behaviour is in agreement with the works of Lefebvre-Devos [8] and Morales et al. [34] but in contrast to that of Hwang et al. [36] and Gao et al. [35], who proposed a two-phase equilibrium mechanism for the lithiation of SnS2. The higher resolution galvanostatic titration experiments from SnS2 up to Li0.48SnS2, along with the results of the Rietveld refinement of the XRD patterns of the electrodes after the titration experiments, could resolve this seeming contradiction as follows: During constant current flux, in which the measured over-potential drives the lithiation reaction, a two-phase electrode constitution consisting of lithium-rich and lithium-poor SnS2 regions may form. However, during equilibration of the electrode in the voltage relaxation step, the lithium-ions are re-distributed within the electrode active material to generate a single solid-solution phase.
